title: 0 · Overture: How Research Works
text:
Where this leads
Straight into Part I · Foundations: Probability & Data — the grammar of uncertainty that every later technique is written in.

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text: Good quantitative research is consistent (reliable), accurate (valid), and reproducible (replicable). When those slip — through selective analysis, hidden choices, and unpublished null results — you get the replication crisis: confident findings that vanish when someone reruns them. This entry is the manual’s conscience, and the reason the chapters ahead are so insistent about rigour.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Why it matters
It is sobering, and clarifying, to learn up front that many celebrated findings in social science don’t replicate. That fact reframes everything that follows: the point of learning statistics rigorously is not to produce significant results, but to produce trustworthy ones. Knowing how research fails is half of knowing how to do it well.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
The criteria for credible research
Three standards, building on the measurement and design ideas you’ve already met (Bryman 2016):

Reliability — consistency. A reliable measure gives the same result on repeated trials: stability (steady over time), internal reliability (a scale’s indicators cohere), and inter-rater reliability (coders agree).
Validity — accuracy (the most important criterion):

Measurement validity — the indicator really reflects the concept (does an IQ test measure intelligence?).
Internal validity — the causal claim is genuine (X truly caused Y, not an unobserved confounder); randomised experiments are the benchmark.
External validity — the result generalises beyond the study’s context.

Replicability vs replication — a distinction worth keeping sharp:

Replicability = the procedures are reported clearly enough that someone could repeat the study.
Replication = someone actually reruns it and checks the findings hold across new samples or times.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
section: The replication crisis — and what causes it
text:
The replication crisis — and what causes it
The replication crisis is the discovery that many widely accepted findings fail to reproduce when rerun (Bryman 2016). Bryman names the mechanisms — and each is a preview of a battle fought later in this manual:

p-hacking — selectively analysing data until something crosses significance. (Made rigorous and exposed in Part II’s hypothesis-testing material.)
The “garden of forking paths” — many individually defensible analytic choices (which cases to drop, which controls to include) lead to different results; flexibility masquerades as discovery.
Publication bias / the “file drawer” — null results go unpublished, so the literature over-represents flukes that happened to reach significance.







WarningThe uncomfortable implication



A single significant result, from a single study, with undisclosed analytic choices, is weak evidence — even when the statistics are flawless. Credibility comes from transparency and repetition, not from a small p-value. Hold this thought; the rest of the manual earns it.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking statistical significance for truth. Significance is a filter that flukes also pass. Replication is the real test.
Reporting too little to be replicable. If no one can rerun your analysis from your description, it isn’t science yet — it’s an anecdote with numbers.
Letting forking paths stay hidden. Every “we tried it this way” you don’t disclose inflates your apparent certainty.
Treating null results as failures. Unpublished nulls are how the literature lies by omission.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — reliability (stability/internal/inter-rater); validity (measurement/internal/external); the replicability-vs-replication distinction; the replication crisis and its causes (p-hacking, the garden of forking paths, publication bias / the file drawer).
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Pre-register when you can. Committing to your analysis before seeing the data is the cheapest defence against p-hacking and forking paths.
Write for the replicator. Document every decision so a stranger could reproduce your result — that discipline also catches your own errors.
Report the nulls. Your “it didn’t work” is someone else’s saved year.
Distrust single studies, including your own. Treat a lone significant finding as a hypothesis, not a conclusion. ## Connections {.unnumbered}
Builds on: measurement (reliability/validity) and research designs (internal/external validity).
Leads to: ethics, the other pillar of doing research responsibly.
Pays off in: Part II · Inference — where p-hacking, power, and “the replication crisis” get their full, rigorous treatment — and Part VI, the mature reckoning with metrics and judgement. ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}

“What practical steps (pre-registration, transparency) do the sources suggest for more credible research?”

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text: A quantitative study is a pipeline: theory → question → hypothesis → design → measurement → data → analysis → conclusion. Every technique in this manual is a tool for one of these steps. Learn the skeleton first, and each later chapter becomes “ah, that’s the part that does step 5.”

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Why it matters
People drown in methods because they learn them as a disconnected toolbox. But methods aren’t a pile — they’re stations on an assembly line. Once you can see the whole line, you always know where you are and what a given technique is for. This entry is that map; the rest of the book fills in each station.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
section: Intuition: research is a sequence, and (almost) everyone agrees on it
text:
Intuition: research is a sequence, and (almost) everyone agrees on it
Two of our sources lay out the sequence explicitly, and they agree far more than they differ.
Bryman’s process of quantitative research — the canonical 12 steps (Bryman 2016):

Theory — the starting point.
Research question(s) — what, precisely, you want to know.
Hypothesis — deduced from theory (sometimes skipped in exploratory work).
Research design — experiment, cross-section, longitudinal, comparative…
Devise measures of concepts — operationalisation.
Select research site(s).
Select respondents/subjects — your sample.
Collect the data — administer the instruments.
Process the data — turn responses into numbers.
Analyse the data — the statistics.
Findings / conclusions.
Write up.

Martin & Bridgmon’s structure — the same arc, tilted toward the statistics (Martin and Bridgmon 2012):

State the problem; identify questions/hypotheses.
Identify, classify, and operationally define the variables.
Choose the research design.
A priori power analysis — work out the sample size you need before collecting data.
Choose the appropriate statistic.
Collect and analyse the data set.
Data screening and analysis (e.g. in SPSS).
Interpret the statistics.
Write the results.







TipRead the two lists together



Bryman emphasises the social-science front end (theory, sites, respondents); Martin & Bridgmon emphasise the statistical spine (operational definitions, power analysis, choosing the right test). The honest model is the union: a study earns its credibility by doing both well. Note step 4 in Martin & Bridgmon — deciding sample size before you collect — is the single most-skipped step in real research, and a recurring villain later in the manual.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
section: A worked study: does therapy reduce adolescent depression?
text:
A worked study: does therapy reduce adolescent depression?
Martin & Bridgmon walk through a study of 145 adolescents that runs the entire pipeline end to end (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) — a perfect first sight of the cycle:

Problem & question. Which is more effective at reducing depressive symptoms — Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Interpersonal Therapy (IPT), or no treatment (control)?
Hypotheses. A null hypothesis H_0: the three groups don’t differ. An alternative: they do.
Design. Three groups, an outcome measured the same way in each.
Power / sample size. Enough adolescents (145) to give the test a fair chance of detecting a real difference.
Choose the statistic. A one-way ANOVA (the right tool for comparing three group means).
Collect & analyse. Run the data through the test.
Decide. The result was p = .003. Because that is below the chosen threshold \alpha = .05, they reject the null and conclude the therapies have genuinely different effects.

That final move — comparing a p-value to a threshold to decide — is the heartbeat of classical inference. You’ll meet it properly in Part II; here, just notice where in the pipeline it sits: step 10 of Bryman, step 8 of Martin & Bridgmon. The statistic is one station, not the whole journey.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking the list for reality. The steps look linear; real studies loop (you revise the question after a pilot, re-operationalise after bad data). The line is a plan, not a promise.
Treating analysis as the whole study. Beginners obsess over step 10 (the statistics) and neglect steps 1–5, where studies are actually won or lost. A perfect ANOVA on a badly operationalised concept is a precise wrong answer.
Skipping the a priori power analysis. Deciding sample size after seeing the data — or not at all — is how underpowered studies and false findings are born.
Letting “write up” hide the messy middle. The clean published pipeline rarely shows the inductive loops that actually happened. Honesty about them is part of rigour.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — the 12-step process of quantitative research.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative and Statistical Research Methods — the 9-step structure (with the a priori power-analysis step); the 145-adolescent CBT/IPT depression study run end to end, including the p = .003 vs \alpha = .05 decision.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Locate yourself on the pipeline, always. When stuck, ask “which step am I on?” Confusion usually means you’ve smuggled a later step’s question into an earlier step.
Do step 5 before step 7. Operationalise your concepts before you choose your sample; you can’t size a study to measure something you haven’t defined.
Run the power analysis as a design check, not a formality. If the honest sample size is unaffordable, you’ve learned something now — before wasting the data collection.
Keep a “loop log.” Note every time reality made you revise an earlier step. It’s the difference between a reproducible study and a story.

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text: You cannot measure “trust,” “depression,” or “social class” directly. You measure a stand-in you’ve defined — and the whole study inherits the quality of that choice. Measurement is the hinge between an idea and a number: get it right and the statistics mean something; get it wrong and you compute precise answers about the wrong thing.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Why it matters
Every technique later in this manual — averages, regressions, causal estimates — operates on variables, not on concepts. If the variable is a poor stand-in for the concept you care about, no amount of sophisticated analysis downstream can rescue it. This is why experienced researchers spend disproportionate effort before any data is collected, on the unglamorous question: how, exactly, will I measure this?
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
From concept to variable: operationalisation
Concepts are the building blocks of theory — the categories (“job satisfaction,” “prejudice,” “political participation”) around which a study is built (Bryman 2016). To investigate one, you must translate it into something researchable and quantifiable: a variable. That translation is operationalisation (Bryman 2016).

An operational definition is the precise recipe for measuring the variable in this study — “the specification of the activities or operations necessary to measure it” (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Example: depressive symptomatology operationally defined as a score on the Beck Depression Inventory.
When a concept isn’t directly quantifiable, you devise indicators to represent it — drawn from survey questions, structured observation, or official statistics (Bryman 2016). Bryman stresses that multiple indicators (e.g. a 5-item Likert scale) usually give more precision than a single question — one item can catch an idiosyncrasy of wording; several average it out.







TipThe hidden choice



Operationalisation is a decision, not a discovery. “Depression = Beck score” and “depression = a clinician’s diagnosis” are different operational definitions that can disagree. Naming your operational definition out loud — and its limits — is the first act of honest quantitative research.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
section: Levels of measurement: what your numbers are allowed to do
text:
Levels of measurement: what your numbers are allowed to do
Once you have a variable, its level of measurement dictates which operations and statistics are even meaningful (Albers 2017; Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Using the wrong ones is a silent, common error.









Level
Property
Examples
Permissible operations




Nominal
Unordered categories; numbers are arbitrary tags
Gender, religion, city
Counts, frequencies, mode. Means are meaningless.


Ordinal
Ordered categories, but unequal/unknown gaps
Likert agreement, race finishing position
Order comparisons, median


Interval
Equal spacing, no true zero
IQ, °Celsius
Add/subtract; differences are meaningful


Ratio
Equal spacing and a true zero
Weight, height, time, count of children
All arithmetic; ratios (“twice as heavy”) are valid









WarningThe Likert trap



Albers warns explicitly: averaging a single Likert item is invalid, because the distance from “Agree” to “Neutral” is not guaranteed equal to “Neutral” to “Disagree” (Albers 2017). The number looks computable, but the scale doesn’t earn the mean. (A multi-item Likert scale is treated more leniently — but that’s a defended choice, not a default.)
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
section: Reliability vs validity: consistency vs accuracy
text:
Reliability vs validity: consistency vs accuracy
These two words are used interchangeably in everyday English but are sharply distinct here — and the relationship between them is one-directional: validity presumes reliability. A measure that is inconsistent cannot be accurate (Bryman 2016).
Reliability — does the measure give the same result on repeated trials? (Consistency.)

Stability (test–retest): the same measure given twice to the same group shouldn’t swing wildly.
Internal reliability: the multiple indicators in a scale should hang together (measure the same thing).
Inter-rater reliability: independent coders should agree — Albers cites Cohen’s Kappa as the standard statistic (Albers 2017).

Validity — does the measure capture the concept you intended? (Accuracy.) The sources distinguish several kinds (Bryman 2016):

Face validity — at minimum, it looks like it measures the concept (to experts).
Criterion validity — it tracks an external benchmark: concurrent (a present criterion like absenteeism) or predictive (a future one).
Construct validity — it behaves as theory says it should; convergent validity checks it against measures of the same thing built by different methods.
Internal validity — confidence the independent variable truly drives the dependent one (causality). External validity — whether results generalise beyond the study; ecological validity — whether they hold in everyday, natural settings.







TipThe dartboard



The classic picture: reliable but not valid = tight cluster of darts, all in the wrong spot (consistent, biased). Valid but not reliable is impossible to sustain — scattered darts can’t be reliably on target. You want tight and centred.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Computing means on ordinal/nominal data. Averaging ranks or category codes produces a number with no meaning. Match the statistic to the measurement level.
Mistaking reliability for validity. A bathroom scale that’s 5 kg heavy is perfectly reliable and perfectly wrong. Consistency is necessary, not sufficient.
Single-indicator fragility. One survey item can be derailed by wording, mood, or translation; multiple indicators are insurance.
Treating operationalisation as obvious. “Just measure income” hides a dozen choices (pre/post tax? household/individual? annual/monthly?) that change the findings.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Decide the measurement level deliberately — it silently constrains every statistic you can later use. Collecting age as bands (ordinal) instead of years (ratio) throws away power you can never recover.
Write the operational definition before you collect anything. If you can’t write it precisely, you don’t yet know what you’re studying.
Check reliability before you trust a result. A finding built on an unreliable measure is noise wearing a number.
Prefer multiple indicators, then verify they cohere (internal reliability) before combining them.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text: Quantitative social science measures and sometimes intervenes in real human lives, so it is bound by commitments that precede any technique: don’t harm, get informed consent, protect privacy, justify any deception. And when you run experiments in the field, a second layer of dilemmas appears — because now your study is changing the world it observes.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Why it matters
Ethics is not a form you sign at the end; it is a design constraint that shapes the study from the start. A finding obtained unethically is not a triumph with an asterisk — it is a failure. Establishing the human-subjects frame here, in the Overture, means every design choice in the chapters ahead carries it by default.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
The core principles
Ethical practice governs the relationship between researchers and the people they study, to prevent exploitation and harm (Bryman 2016):

Informed consent. Participants get enough information — purpose, risks, and the voluntary nature of taking part — to decide freely, usually documented with an information sheet and consent form.
Avoidance of harm. No physical, psychological, or social harm: protect participants from stress, loss of self-esteem, or being induced to do something reprehensible.
Privacy and confidentiality. Keep records secure and ensure individuals are not identifiable in anything published.
Deception. Representing the research as something it isn’t is sometimes used to elicit natural responses, but it is highly controversial, demands strong justification, and requires debriefing afterward.

The role of ethics review (IRB). Most institutions require an Institutional Review Board to evaluate proposed research: it weighs risks, checks confidentiality safeguards, and ensures participants can withdraw. A common yardstick is the minimal-risk threshold — is the anticipated harm no greater than that of ordinary daily life? (Bryman 2016)

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
section: The extra dilemmas of field experiments
text:
The extra dilemmas of field experiments
Field experiments raise problems lab studies don’t, because they intervene in real settings where people may not know they’re in a study (Gerber and Green 2012). Gerber & Green work through several:

Protecting the integrity of random assignment. When you collaborate with agencies (police, schools), well-meaning practitioners may try to “help” the neediest by slipping them into the treatment group — subverting randomisation and destroying the study’s logic.
The “no-harm” design principle: encourage, don’t deny. Induce what you believe to be helpful treatments. Studying SAT-prep benefits? Randomly encourage people to take the class — don’t randomly prevent willing people from taking it.
Stepped-wedge designs to protect the control group. When a treatment is expected to help, give it to everyone eventually but randomise the timing; those still waiting serve as the controls. No one is permanently denied a beneficial treatment.
Informed-consent waivers. Where informing people would destroy the very “naturalness” being studied, an IRB may waive consent — if risk is minimal and the research couldn’t otherwise be done.
Third-party responses. Other actors can react to your experiment. If an NGO notices you’ve placed female leaders in certain villages and redirects its aid to male-led ones, it both contaminates the study and raises a fairness question about who gets help.
Tangible vs intangible harm. Judging harm is hard when the outcome is beliefs or opinions (e.g. nudging someone’s political or religious views) rather than physical or financial loss.







TipEthics as design, not paperwork



Notice that every field-experiment safeguard is a design move — encourage-don’t-deny, stepped-wedge timing, protecting randomisation. Ethics and good design are the same craft seen from two angles: a study that treats people well is usually a study that yields cleaner inferences.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Consent as a checkbox. A signed form without genuine understanding isn’t informed consent.
Deception without debriefing. If you must mislead, you must explain afterward — skipping that is indefensible.
Letting practitioners “fix” the assignment. Compassionate tampering with randomisation is still tampering; it destroys the causal claim and the fairness rationale.
Ignoring third-party contamination. Outside actors reacting to your treatment can wreck both ethics and validity at once.
Denying a beneficial treatment to a control group when a stepped-wedge design would have given it to everyone eventually.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what

Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — protecting the integrity of random assignment; the encourage-don’t-deny “no-harm” principle (SAT-prep example); stepped-wedge designs; consent waivers under minimal risk; third-party responses (the NGO/village-leaders example); tangible vs intangible harm.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Design the ethics in from day one. Ask “who could this harm, and how would I know?” while choosing the design, not after.
Prefer encouragement designs. Randomly offering a likely-beneficial treatment is almost always more defensible than randomly withholding one.
Reach for the stepped wedge whenever the treatment is expected to help — it dissolves the “control group is denied benefit” objection.
Engage the IRB early and honestly. Treat review as a design partner, not a hurdle; surface third-party and intangible-harm risks before they surface you. ## Connections {.unnumbered}
Builds on: research designs — especially experiments, where most ethical force concentrates.

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text: A research design is the skeleton of a study: when you collect data, from whom, and with what comparison. It sets a hard ceiling on the strength of any conclusion you can draw. Choose it before you analyse anything, because the most sophisticated statistics in the world cannot buy back a causal claim a cross-sectional design was never able to make.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Why it matters
Beginners think credibility comes from the analysis. It comes from the design. The design determines whether you can say “X causes Y” or only “X and Y go together.” This entry is the map of the main designs and what each can — and cannot — license. It’s also the bridge to the summit of the manual: causal inference is, at heart, the art of designing (or reconstructing) a fair comparison.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: The designs, and what each buys you
text:
The designs, and what each buys you
A research design reflects the priority you place on causal connection, generalisation, and understanding change over time (Bryman 2016).

Experimental
Manipulate an independent variable and use random assignment to make the treatment and control groups comparable.

Strengths: the benchmark for internal validity — it rules out alternative causal explanations. Field experiments, run in real settings, add ecological validity.
Limits: many social variables (gender, ethnicity, class) can’t be manipulated for ethical or practical reasons; lab experiments can suffer reactive effects when people know they’re studied, hurting generalisability.



Cross-sectional / survey
Data on many cases at a single point in time, to find patterns of association.

Strengths: ideal for non-manipulable variables (age, background); with random probability sampling, strong external validity.
Limits: weak internal validity — everything is measured at once, so the direction of causation is ambiguous. It shows relationships, not causes.



Longitudinal / panel
The same sample studied on ≥2 occasions (panel = same people; cohort = people sharing a trait, e.g. birth week).

Strengths: establishes temporal order, enabling stronger causal inference than cross-sectional designs; essential for charting social change.
Limits: expensive and slow; suffers attrition as participants drop out, which can make the survivors unrepresentative.



Comparative
Two or more contrasting cases (e.g. nations) studied with near-identical methods.

Strengths: reveals how a phenomenon varies across socio-cultural settings, guarding against findings that are culturally specific.
Limits: hard to ensure data are truly comparable across contexts (translation of instruments is a classic trap).



Case study
Intensive analysis of a single case — a community, organisation, or person.

Strengths: deep, idiographic insight; supports analytic generalisation (to theory, not population).
Limits: weak external validity — one case rarely represents a wider population.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: The two rulers: internal vs external validity
text:
The two rulers: internal vs external validity
Every design is judged on two different axes, and they often trade off:

Internal validity — confidence the IV genuinely caused the change in the DV (Bryman 2016). Experiments win (a control group neutralises threats like history and testing effects); cross-sectional designs are weak (association ≠ sequence).
External validity — whether results generalise beyond the study (Bryman 2016). Large random-sample surveys win; lab experiments often lose (artificial settings, unrepresentative participants — frequently students). Field experiments sit in the sweet spot: real participants and contexts give them stronger external validity than the lab while keeping randomisation’s internal validity.







TipThere’s usually no free lunch



The lab maximises internal validity at the cost of external; the survey does the reverse. Much of research craft is choosing which validity your question can’t live without, and paying the other’s price knowingly. Field experiments are prized precisely because they compromise least.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: Why the randomised experiment is the benchmark
text:
Why the randomised experiment is the benchmark
When you can run one, randomisation is a near-magical tool — it is the standard every other design is measured against (Gerber and Green 2012). It works because random assignment makes treatment statistically independent of everything about the subjects:

Comparable groups / a fair test. No systematic advantage to treatment or control; with a large enough sample, individual differences wash out (Gerber and Green 2012).
Eliminates confounders — even unobserved ones. Randomisation severs the link between treatment and every variable that predicts the outcome, solving the lurking-variable problem that plagues observational research (Gerber and Green 2012). This is the part no statistical “control” can match: you can only control for confounders you thought of and measured; randomisation handles the ones you didn’t.
Unbiased estimation. Replicated infinitely, the average estimate equals the true treatment effect (Gerber and Green 2012).

Concrete example — women heading Indian village councils. Because seats were randomly reserved for female leaders (by law), any difference in, say, sanitation spending between those councils and male-led ones could be attributed to the leader’s gender rather than to pre-existing village differences (Gerber and Green 2012). Randomisation turned an unanswerable observational question into a credible causal one. (You’ll meet this study’s statistics again in sampling distributions.)
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Causal claims from cross-sectional data. “X correlates with Y at one time point” is not “X causes Y.” This is the most common over-reach in applied social science.
Ignoring attrition in panels. If drop-out is related to the outcome, your surviving sample silently biases the result.
Generalising a case study to a population. Case studies generalise to theory, not to people-in-general.
Trusting a lab result in the wild. High internal validity doesn’t imply the effect survives outside the artificial setting.
Assuming you can always randomise. Often you can’t (ethics, law, practicality) — which is exactly why Part V exists: to approximate the experiment you couldn’t run.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — the five designs and their strengths/limits; internal vs external (and ecological) validity; how each design fares on each.

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Start from the conclusion you need, then pick the design. If you need “X causes Y,” a survey won’t do — design for the claim, don’t retrofit it.
State your validity priority explicitly. Write down whether internal or external validity matters more for this decision; it resolves most design arguments.
When you can’t randomise, say so — and switch to causal-inference tools. The honest move is to name the missing experiment and adopt a design (Part V) that approximates it.
Budget for attrition up front in any longitudinal study; plan retention like you plan sampling.

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text: Quantitative social science is the disciplined attempt to test ideas about human behaviour using numbers: you turn a fuzzy concept into something you can measure, collect data on it, and let the evidence confirm or overturn what you believed. The numbers are not the point — deciding what to count, and what the count is allowed to prove, is the point.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Why it matters
Every technique in this manual — probability, regression, causal inference — is a tool. Before tools, you need the job: what are we actually trying to do when we “do quantitative research”? Get this wrong and you produce numbers that look authoritative and mean nothing. Get it right and even a simple average can settle a real question. This entry is the frame that gives all the later machinery a purpose.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Intuition: research as a strategy, not a calculation
text:
Intuition: research as a strategy, not a calculation
Quantitative research is best understood not as “doing maths on data” but as a systematic strategy that prioritises quantification in order to test theories (Bryman 2016). Bryman characterises it through three orientations that travel together:

Deductive — theory comes first; you test it. (More on this below.)
Positivist — you study social reality using the methods of the natural sciences.
Objectivist — you treat social facts as real, external things that exist independently of the observer.

Martin & Bridgmon frame the same enterprise more operationally, as the discipline of managing variance — their “MaxMinCon” principle: Maximise the variance due to the thing you’re studying, Minimise error variance, and Control the extraneous variance from everything else (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). That single idea — make the signal big, the noise small, and the confounds held still — is the hidden agenda behind nearly every method later in this book.

Quantitative vs qualitative: a question of stance
Quantitative isn’t “better” than qualitative research; it answers different questions from a different stance. Bryman contrasts them along several dimensions (Bryman 2016):









Quantitative
Qualitative




Medium
Numbers, measurement
Words, images


Whose view counts
The researcher’s (you decide what to measure)
The participants’ (seen through their eyes)


Relationship to subjects
Distant, sometimes no contact at all
Close involvement


Picture of social life
A static snapshot of relationships
An unfolding process


Scale
Large-scale trends (macro)
Small-scale interactions (micro)



The trade is real: quantification buys you scale, comparability, and a way to quantify uncertainty, at the cost of closeness and context. Knowing which you need is the first research decision you ever make.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Deductive and inductive: how theory and data talk to each other
text:
Deductive and inductive: how theory and data talk to each other
The engine of research is the relationship between theory and data, and it runs in two directions.

Deductive (theory → data). You start from what’s known and relevant theory, deduce a hypothesis, and test it against evidence. Theory drives what you collect (Bryman 2016). Martin & Bridgmon call this the hypothetico-deductive model: general theory → specific observations → confirmation or rejection (Martin and Bridgmon 2012).
Inductive (data → theory). Theory is the outcome: you observe, then draw generalisable inferences that become new theoretical ideas (Bryman 2016).







TipThe honest version: it’s a spiral, not a line



Both sources stress these are tendencies, not boxes. Deductive studies contain “inductive loops” where theory is revised after the findings come in; Martin & Bridgmon call the real process the inductive–hypothetico–deductive spiral (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). In practice you loop: theory shapes data, data reshapes theory, repeat.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Worked examples: the two directions in real studies
text:
Worked examples: the two directions in real studies
Deductive — do immigrants’ gender attitudes converge on their host country’s? Bryman uses Röder & Mühlau (2014) (Röder and Mühlau 2014). They began from established theory (national gender attitudes differ; migrants move between cultures), deduced five hypotheses predicting that second-generation immigrants adopt host-country gender values, and confirmed them using European Social Survey data — evidence of an “intergenerational acculturation process.” Theory first, data as the test.
Inductive — why is front-line customer service so poor? Bryman uses O’Reilly et al. (2012) (O’Reilly et al. 2012). The researcher started with open-ended observation and interviews of retail employees — no hypothesis. The concept of “service silos” (management structures that quietly constrained employees) emerged from the data as the theory that explained what she saw. Data first, theory as the product.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating deductive vs inductive as a rigid choice. Real research spirals between them; pretending you “only test hypotheses” hides the inductive judgement you actually used.
Forgetting the positivist/objectivist assumptions. Treating a measured construct as if it were an objective external fact — when it’s a contestable operationalisation — is the original sin that later resurfaces as the tyranny of metrics.
Forcing numbers onto a qualitative question. If the real question is about meaning, context, or process, quantification can produce precise answers to the wrong question.
Skipping “MaxMinCon” thinking. A study that doesn’t deliberately maximise signal, minimise error, and control confounds is usually measuring noise.

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Name your direction out loud. Before designing anything, say whether you’re testing a theory or building one. It changes everything downstream — sampling, measurement, how you read a surprising result.
Write the conclusion you’re afraid of. State, in advance, the finding that would prove you wrong. If your design can’t produce it, you’re not testing — you’re decorating.
“MaxMinCon” is a design checklist. For any study ask: what’s my signal, what’s my error, what are my confounds — and what in the design handles each?

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text: You don’t meet a new distribution for every problem — you meet the same handful, over and over, each encoding a particular story about how chance operates. Two outcomes? Bernoulli. Counting successes? Binomial. Rare events in an interval? Poisson. Waiting for something? Exponential. Many small effects adding up? Normal. Knowing the stories lets you recognise the right model instead of guessing.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Why it matters
A named distribution is a massive shortcut: identify it and you instantly inherit its mean, its variance, its shape, and a century of results. But the deeper skill is reading the story — the generative assumptions each one encodes — because that tells you when the model fits and, more importantly, when it doesn’t. The chapter’s recurring warning (Bruce & Bruce) lives here: the Normal distribution is so iconic that people reach for it reflexively, yet real social and economic data is routinely long-tailed, and forcing it into a bell underestimates exactly the extreme events that matter most.
These seven also wire directly into later chapters: the Binomial underlies proportions and A/B tests, the Normal underlies the CLT and nearly all classical inference, the Poisson and Exponential underlie event and duration models.

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
The discrete stories
Bernoulli — the atom. A single trial with two outcomes, “success” (1) and “failure” (0) (Ross 2018). It’s the simplest possible random variable and the building block of everything below. Bruce & Bruce note that binary outcomes — buy/don’t buy, fraud/not fraud, click/no click — sit at the heart of business analytics (Bruce et al. 2020).
Binomial — counting successes. Add up n independent Bernoulli trials, each with the same success probability p, and you get the number of successes (Ross 2018). Its mean is np and variance np(1-p) — note variance is largest at p=0.5 (maximum uncertainty) and vanishes at p=0 or 1.

Worked examples. Ross: in 5 fair coin flips, P(\text{exactly 2 heads}) = \binom{5}{2}
(\tfrac12)^5 = 10/32. A quality-control case: screws defective with p = 0.01 — the probability a 10-screw package needs replacement (more than 1 defective) is \approx 0.004 (Ross 2018). Bruce: with a 2% conversion rate, the chance of 0 sales in 200 clicks (Bruce et al. 2020).

Poisson — rare events in an interval. Count events that occur at a constant average rate \lambda over a fixed span of time or space, each independent of the last (Ross 2018). Its signature: mean equals variance, both \lambda.

Worked examples. Ross: typographical errors on a page (\lambda = 0.5), or alpha particles emitted by a radioactive source (\lambda = 3.2) (Ross 2018). Bruce: queuing and capacity — how much server capacity to handle web traffic arriving in 5-second windows (Bruce et al. 2020).

Geometric — waiting for the first success. How many independent trials until success first strikes (Ross 2018). It is memoryless (the discrete version): past failures don’t make success any more “due” — a direct echo of the gambler’s fallacy.

Worked examples. Ross: the expected number of fair-die rolls to get a “1” is 1/p = 6; drawing balls from an urn of N white and M black until the first black (Ross 2018).
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
The continuous stories
Uniform — featureless ignorance. Every value in (\alpha,\beta) equally likely; the density is flat (Ross 2018). The model for “could be anywhere in this range.”

Worked example (Ross). A passenger arrives at a bus stop where buses come every 15 minutes; if their arrival is uniform between 7:00 and 7:30, the waiting-time probabilities are areas of rectangles (Ross 2018).

Normal — the sum of many small effects. The bell curve, symmetric about \mu, with spread \sigma. Its story is the deep one: a quantity shaped by many small, independent, additive influences tends to be Normal (Ross 2018) — which is precisely the Central Limit Theorem, and why the Normal is the most important distribution in statistics. The 68–95–99.7 rule is worth memorising: about 68% of the mass lies within \pm 1\sigma of the mean, 95% within \pm 2\sigma, and 99.7% within \pm 3\sigma (Ross 2018).

Worked examples. Ross: the heights of 25-year-old men, \mu = 71 inches, \sigma = 2.5 (Ross 2018). Bruce: regression errors and body temperatures (Bruce et al. 2020).

Exponential — time until an event. The waiting time to the next event in a Poisson process (Ross 2018). Its defining trait is the memoryless property: the chance the event happens in the next hour is the same no matter how long you’ve already waited — the distribution doesn’t age (Ross 2018).

Worked examples. Ross: time until the next earthquake, or until someone dials a wrong number (Ross 2018). Bruce: time between website visits, or until an electronic component fails (Bruce et al. 2020).
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Two approximations that connect them
The distributions aren’t isolated — they bleed into one another in limits, which is both beautiful and practically useful.
Binomial → Poisson (rare events). When n is large and p small with np = \lambda moderate, the Binomial converges to the Poisson (Ross 2018). The engine is the limit (1 - \lambda/n)^n \to e^{-\lambda}. Use it when you know the rate \lambda but can’t pin down n and p separately — exactly the situation for “rare events at a known average rate.”
Binomial → Normal (De Moivre–Laplace). When n is large, the Binomial is approximately Normal with mean np and variance np(1-p) (Ross 2018). Ross’s rule of thumb: trust it once np(1-p) \ge 10. Because the Normal is continuous and the Binomial is discrete, apply a continuity correction — treat P\{X = 20\} as P\{19.5 < X < 20.5\} (Ross 2018; Bruce et al. 2020). This is a special case of the CLT (a Binomial is a sum of Bernoullis), and a preview of why the bell shows up everywhere.


Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import comb

def binom_pmf(n, p):
    ks = np.arange(0, n + 1)
    pmf = np.array([comb(n, int(k)) * p**k * (1 - p)**(n - k) for k in ks])
    return ks, pmf

p = 0.5
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.2))
for ax, n in zip(axes, [5, 15, 50]):
    ks, pmf = binom_pmf(n, p)
    ax.bar(ks, pmf, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8, width=0.9)
    mu, sigma = n * p, np.sqrt(n * p * (1 - p))
    xs = np.linspace(0, n, 300)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=2)
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
fig.suptitle("A Binomial becomes a bell — De Moivre–Laplace in action", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The Binomial (bars) and its Normal approximation (curve) for p = 0.5 as n grows. By n = 50 the match is excellent — the De Moivre–Laplace theorem, and a concrete glimpse of the CLT.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
section: The crucial caveat: real data isn’t Normal
text:
The crucial caveat: real data isn’t Normal
Bruce & Bruce sound a warning that the rest of this book takes seriously: the Normal distribution is iconic, but raw data in the real world is rarely Normal (Bruce et al. 2020). Incomes, city sizes, firm revenues, financial returns, social-media activity — these are long-tailed, with extreme “black swan” values far more likely than a bell predicts (Bruce et al. 2020). Modelling such data as Normal systematically understates the risk of the very outliers that dominate outcomes.






WarningThe Normal is for averages, not raw data



Reconcile this with the previous section: the CLT promises that averages and sums tend to Normal, not that your individual observations are. A histogram of incomes is sharply right-skewed; a histogram of mean incomes across many samples is bell-shaped. Confusing the two is the single most common modelling error in applied work — see the CLT article’s pitfalls.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming Normality for raw data. Especially for money, counts, and durations, which are skewed or long-tailed. Plot the data first.
Using the Normal approximation when np(1-p) is small. For rare events (p tiny), reach for the Poisson, not the Normal — the bell approximation is poor in the tail you care about.
Forgetting the continuity correction. Approximating a discrete Binomial by a continuous Normal without the \pm 0.5 adjustment biases small-count probabilities.
Misreading the Poisson’s independence assumption. Events must occur at a constant rate, independently. Clustered events (contagion, bursts) violate it and need richer models.
Trusting the memoryless property where it doesn’t hold. Human waiting times (a bus on a schedule, a machine that wears out) are often not memoryless — the Exponential assumes no aging, which real systems frequently break.

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — all seven distributions with their stories, pmfs/pdfs, means, and variances; the worked examples (5 coins → 10/32; defective screws ≈ 0.004; typos \lambda=0.5 and alpha particles \lambda=3.2; expected 6 die rolls; bus-stop uniform; men’s heights \mu=71,\sigma=2.5; earthquakes/wrong numbers); the 68–95–99.7 rule; the Binomial→Poisson limit via (1-\lambda/n)^n\to e^{-\lambda}; the De Moivre–Laplace Binomial→Normal approximation with the np(1-p)\ge 10 rule and continuity correction.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — which distributions matter in practice (Binomial for business analytics; Poisson/Exponential for queuing and simulation); the conversion-rate and server-traffic examples; and the central warning that real data is long-tailed, not Normal.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Identify the story, then the distribution. Two outcomes → Bernoulli; counting them → Binomial; rare counts per interval → Poisson; waiting → Geometric/Exponential; many small additive effects → Normal. The story picks the model.
Plot before you assume. A five-minute histogram tells you whether “Normal” is a fantasy. For money, counts, and durations, expect skew and long tails.
Match the approximation to the regime. Large n, moderate p → Normal; large n, tiny p → Poisson. Don’t Normal-approximate rare events.
Remember mean = variance flags a Poisson. If a count’s variance roughly equals its mean, Poisson is a reasonable start; if variance far exceeds the mean (overdispersion), it isn’t.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Bernoulli moments. E[X] = 1\cdot p + 0\cdot(1-p) = p; E[X^2] = p, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = p - p^2 = p(1-p).
Binomial via Bernoulli sums. Writing X = \sum_{k=1}^n B_k with independent Bernoulli(p) B_k, linearity gives E[X] = np, and independence (variances add) gives \operatorname{Var}(X) = np(1-p) — no need to sum the pmf directly.
Binomial → Poisson. With \lambda = np fixed as n\to\infty, 
\binom{n}{i}p^i(1-p)^{n-i} = \frac{n!}{i!(n-i)!}\Big(\frac{\lambda}{n}\Big)^i\Big(1-\frac{\lambda}{n}\Big)^{n-i}
\;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\lambda^i e^{-\lambda}}{i!},
 using \big(1-\tfrac{\lambda}{n}\big)^n \to e^{-\lambda} and \tfrac{n!}{(n-i)!\,n^i}\to 1.
Poisson moments. E[X] = \sum_{i\ge 0} i\,\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^i}{i!} = \lambda; a similar sum gives E[X(X-1)] = \lambda^2, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 + \lambda -
\lambda^2 = \lambda — mean and variance coincide.
Exponential memorylessness. P\{X > s+t \mid X > s\} = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda
s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P\{X > t\} — the past is forgotten. The geometric satisfies the discrete analogue.
Normal moments and the 68–95–99.7 rule follow from \int \phi = 1 and Gaussian integration; the empirical rule is \Phi(1)-\Phi(-1)\approx 0.6826, \Phi(2)-\Phi(-2)\approx 0.9544, \Phi(3)-\Phi(-3)\approx 0.9974.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text: The classical formula P(E) = |E|/|S| turns every probability into two counts: the favourable outcomes over the total. The trouble is that real sample spaces are huge — a 9-person batting order has 362,880 arrangements — so you must count without enumerating. Three tools do almost all the work: the multiplication principle, permutations (order matters), and combinations (order doesn’t).

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Why it matters
This is the most mechanical entry in the chapter, and the most quietly important. The combination count \binom{n}{r} you meet here is the same \binom{n}{r} that sits at the front of the binomial distribution in P7, which in turn drives the analysis of yes/no survey data, polling, and A/B tests. Sampling with and without replacement — the backbone of survey sampling — is a counting question. Learn to count cleanly now and several later results arrive pre-assembled.
The deeper habit is the one from the De Méré story: intuition about “how many ways” is unreliable, and a small slip in the count becomes a wrong probability. The tools below replace guessing with bookkeeping.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
The multiplication principle
The foundation: if one choice can be made in m ways and a second independent choice in n ways, the two together can be made in m \times n ways. Extend it to any number of stages by multiplying across them (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997).

Worked example (Grinstead & Snell). A meal has 2 appetiser choices, 3 main courses, and 2 desserts. The number of distinct meals is 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 12 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).

This single idea — multiply the choices at each stage — generates both of the formulas below; they are just the multiplication principle applied to specific selection problems.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Permutations — when order matters
A permutation is an ordered arrangement. To order n distinct objects, you have n choices for the first slot, n-1 for the second, and so on — so by the multiplication principle there are 
n! = n \times (n-1) \times \cdots \times 2 \times 1
 arrangements (Ross 2018).

Worked example (Ross). A baseball team of 9 players can bat in 9! = 362{,}880 different orders (Ross 2018).

If you arrange only r of the n objects (still caring about order), you stop after r slots: 
{}_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!} = n \times (n-1) \times \cdots \times (n-r+1).

When objects repeat, some orderings become indistinguishable, and you divide them out:

Worked example (Ross). The word PEPPER has 6 letters, but with 3 P’s and 2 E’s identical, the number of distinguishable arrangements is 
\frac{6!}{3!\,2!\,1!} = 60,
 not 6! = 720 (Ross 2018). Dividing by 3! and 2! removes the rearrangements of identical letters that don’t produce a new “word.”

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Combinations — when order doesn’t matter
A combination is an unordered selection of r objects from n. Because order no longer distinguishes selections, you take the ordered count {}_nP_r and divide by the r! orderings of each chosen group: 
\binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!},
 read “n choose r” (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997). This is the binomial coefficient.

Worked examples.

Choosing a committee of 5 from 10 people: \binom{10}{5} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7
\cdot 6}{5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 252 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).
Choosing a committee of 3 from 20 people: \binom{20}{3} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 19 \cdot 18}{3
\cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 1{,}140 (Ross 2018).








TipThe one question that picks the tool



Before counting, ask: does order matter? A batting order, a ranked podium, a PIN — order matters, use permutations. A committee, a hand of cards, a random sample of respondents — order is irrelevant, use combinations. Most counting mistakes are really this-question-answered-wrong: counting arrangements when you wanted selections (overcounting by a factor of r!), or vice versa.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Why social scientists care: sampling
Drawing a sample of n people from a population of N — without replacement and without regard to order — is exactly a combination: there are \binom{N}{n} possible samples. Every statement that a simple random sample is “equally likely to be any subset” rests on this count, and the machinery of sampling distributions (the flagship CLT article) is built on top of it. Counting is not a detour from social science; it is the hidden floor under survey inference.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using permutations when order is irrelevant. Counting ordered arrangements for an unordered selection overcounts by exactly r!. A “committee” is a combination, not a permutation.
Forgetting to divide out repeats. Treating PEPPER’s identical letters as distinct inflates the count more than tenfold (720 vs 60). Whenever objects are interchangeable, divide by the factorials of their multiplicities.
Confusing with- and without-replacement. Sampling the same person twice (with replacement) and sampling two different people (without) give different counts and different probabilities — be explicit about which the design uses.
Multiplying when you should add (or vice versa). Multiply across independent stages of a single arrangement (“and”); add across mutually exclusive cases (“or”). Mixing these up is the structural error beneath most wrong counts.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the basic principle of counting; permutations n! (the 9-player batting order, 362{,}880); permutations with indistinguishable objects (PEPPER, 60); combinations \binom{n}{r} (committee of 3 from 20, 1{,}140).
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the multiplication principle (the 2 × 3 × 2 = 12 meal example); combinations (committee of 5 from 10, 252).

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Decide “order or not?” before touching a formula. That single question selects permutation vs combination and prevents the most common error outright.
Translate “and” → multiply, “or” → add. Stages joined by “and” multiply; mutually exclusive cases joined by “or” add. Narrate the problem and the arithmetic follows.
Use the complement when “at least one” appears. Counting the complement (“none”) is usually far smaller than counting every favourable case directly — the same trick as in the axioms entry.
Recognise \binom{n}{r} when it returns. The same coefficient reappears in the binomial distribution and Pascal’s triangle; spotting it early makes P7 feel like review rather than new material.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Permutations of r from n. By the multiplication principle, the number of ways to fill r ordered slots from n distinct objects is 
{}_nP_r = n(n-1)\cdots(n-r+1) = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}.

Combinations from permutations. Each unordered selection of r objects can be ordered in r! ways, so {}_nP_r = r! \binom{n}{r}, giving 
\binom{n}{r} = \frac{{}_nP_r}{r!} = \frac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!}.

Symmetry and Pascal’s rule. Directly from the formula, 
\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n}{n-r}, \qquad
\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n-1}{r-1} + \binom{n-1}{r}.
 The second identity (Pascal’s rule) is the engine of Pascal’s triangle: choosing r from n either includes a fixed element (then choose r-1 from the rest) or excludes it (choose r from the rest).
The binomial theorem. The same coefficients expand powers of a sum: 
(x + y)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} x^{r} y^{\,n-r},
 because forming an x^r y^{n-r} term means choosing which r of the n factors contribute an x — a combination. This is exactly why \binom{n}{r} heads the binomial distribution in P7: it counts the number of sequences with r successes.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Connections

Builds on: Sample spaces & the axioms — counting is how you evaluate P(E) = |E|/|S| when outcomes are equally likely.
Leads to: Conditional probability, where counting restricted sample spaces becomes second nature.
Pays off in: the common distributions (the binomial coefficient is the heart of the binomial distribution) and survey sampling (a random sample is a combination).

title: Expectation & Variance
text: A distribution can be complicated; two summaries do most of the work. The expectation is its balance point — the long-run average. The variance is its scatter — how far values stray from that balance. Master two algebraic facts about them — expectation is linear (always), variance of a sum adds only when things are independent — and the standard error, the heart of inference, falls out for free.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Why it matters
Expectation and variance are the atoms of quantitative reasoning. “The average treatment effect,” “the mean income,” “the expected return” — all expectations. “The standard deviation,” “the margin of error,” “the volatility” — all built from variance. More than that: the single most important formula in applied statistics, the standard error \text{SE}(\bar X) = \sigma/\sqrt{n} that powers the Central Limit Theorem, is just the variance-of-a-sum rule applied to an average. This entry is where that formula is earned.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Expectation: the balance point
The expected value E[X] is a weighted average of the values X can take, each weighted by its probability (Ross 2018): 
E[X] = \sum_x x\,p(x) \quad\text{(discrete)}, \qquad E[X] = \int x\,f(x)\,dx \quad\text{(continuous)}.

Two intuitions, both worth carrying:

Long-run average. Repeat the experiment endlessly and the running average of outcomes settles on E[X] (Ross 2018). (That this must happen is the Law of Large Numbers.)
Centre of gravity. Place a weight equal to each probability at its value along a rod; E[X] is the point where the rod balances (Ross 2018). Skew the weights right and the balance point slides right.


Worked examples (Ross).

Three fair coins, Y = number of heads. The masses are p(0,1,2,3) =
\tfrac18,\tfrac38,\tfrac38,\tfrac18, so E[Y] = 0\cdot\tfrac18 + 1\cdot\tfrac38 + 2\cdot\tfrac38 + 3\cdot\tfrac18 = 1.5. Note the expectation, 1.5, is a value Y can never take — it’s a balance point, not a forecast (Ross 2018).
Uniform on (\alpha,\beta). With constant density, the balance point is the midpoint, E[X] = \dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{2} (Ross 2018).

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Linearity of expectation — the workhorse
For any random variables X, Y and constants a, b (Ross 2018): 
E[aX + bY] = a\,E[X] + b\,E[Y].







TipWhy linearity needs no independence



This is the most useful fact in the chapter, and its power is that it holds even when X and Y are dependent (Ross 2018). The reason is mechanical: E[X+Y] sums the quantity (x+y) against the joint distribution P\{X=x, Y=y\}. That double sum splits into a sum of the x-part and a sum of the y-part, and each collapses to a marginal — \sum_x x\,P\{X=x\} and \sum_y y\,P\{Y=y\} — regardless of how X and Y are related. Dependence rearranges where the joint mass sits, but it can’t change the marginal totals. (Dependence does matter for the variance of a sum — just not the expectation.)


Linearity lets you compute the mean of a complicated sum without ever finding its distribution — the trick behind the unbiasedness of the sample mean, E[\bar X] = \mu, used in the flagship CLT article.

title: Expectation & Variance
section: LOTUS: the mean of a function, the lazy way
text:
LOTUS: the mean of a function, the lazy way
You often want E[g(X)] — the expected value of some transformation, like X^2. The Law of the Unconscious Statistician says you can compute it directly from X’s distribution, without first deriving the distribution of g(X) (Ross 2018): 
E[g(X)] = \sum_x g(x)\,p(x) \quad\text{or}\quad \int g(x)\,f(x)\,dx.


Worked example (Ross). Let X take values -1, 0, 1 with probabilities 0.2, 0.5, 0.3. Then E[X^2] = (-1)^2(0.2) + 0^2(0.5) + 1^2(0.3) = 0.5 — just reweight the squared values (Ross 2018). (Note E[X^2] = 0.5 \ne (E[X])^2 = 0.01; squaring and averaging don’t commute — exactly the gap the variance measures.)

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Variance: the scatter
The variance is the expected squared distance from the mean (Ross 2018): 
\operatorname{Var}(X) = E\big[(X-\mu)^2\big] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2, \qquad \mu = E[X].
 The second form (via LOTUS) is the one you actually compute with. The standard deviation \operatorname{SD}(X) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)} returns the scatter to the original units (squared dollars back to dollars).
If E[X] is the centre of gravity, variance is the moment of inertia — how far the distribution’s “mass” sits from the centre (Ross 2018). Same balance point, very different spread:

Worked examples (Ross).

Fair die. E[X] = 3.5 and E[X^2] = \tfrac{1+4+9+16+25+36}{6} = \tfrac{91}{6}, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = \tfrac{91}{6} - 3.5^2 = \tfrac{35}{12} \approx 2.917 (Ross 2018).
Same mean, different spread. Let W \equiv 0; Y = \pm 1 with probability \tfrac12 each; Z = \pm 100 with probability \tfrac12 each. All three have mean 0, yet \operatorname{Var}(W) = 0, \operatorname{Var}(Y) = 1, \operatorname{Var}(Z) = 10{,}000 — identical centres, wildly different scatter (Ross 2018). The mean alone never tells you the spread.

title: Expectation & Variance
section: The variance of a sum — and the standard error
text:
The variance of a sum — and the standard error
Unlike expectation, variance does not add freely. For two variables, 
\operatorname{Var}(X + Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X, Y).
 The covariance term is exactly where dependence re-enters (the extraction’s “correlation only affects the variance of a sum, not its expectation”) (Ross 2018). When X and Y are independent, the covariance is 0 and variances simply add — the fact that unlocks the standard error.






TipWhere SE = σ/√n comes from



Take an average of n independent draws, each with variance \sigma^2. Variances of independent things add, and the constant 1/n pulls out squared: 
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \operatorname{Var}\!\Big(\tfrac1n \textstyle\sum_i X_i\Big)
= \tfrac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \operatorname{Var}(X_i) = \tfrac{1}{n^2}(n\sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
 Take the square root: \operatorname{SE}(\bar X) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}. The entire \sqrt{n} law of precision — quadruple the sample to halve the error — is this one line. It is why the CLT chapter can promise that averages get sharp.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating the expectation as a likely value. E[Y] = 1.5 heads is the balance point, not a prediction — the variable may never take it. Report spread alongside the mean.
Assuming E[g(X)] = g(E[X]). False in general (Jensen’s inequality). E[X^2] \ne (E[X])^2 is the very gap that defines variance. Squaring then averaging \ne averaging then squaring.
Adding variances that aren’t independent. \operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X)
+ \operatorname{Var}(Y) holds only when \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0. Forgetting the covariance term is how people understate uncertainty for correlated data.
Reporting variance in squared units. Variance is in units^2; convert to the standard deviation before interpreting “spread” in plain language.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — expectation as a probability-weighted average, with the long-run-average and centre-of-gravity intuitions (three-coins E[Y]=1.5; uniform midpoint); linearity of expectation for any X,Y and the joint-distribution argument for why independence isn’t needed; LOTUS (X\in\{-1,0,1\}, E[X^2]=0.5); variance as E[(X-\mu)^2]=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2 with the moment-of-inertia intuition (fair die \operatorname{Var}=35/12; the W/Y/Z same-mean/different-spread trio); variance of a sum and the role of covariance.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Compute means with linearity, not brute force. Break a complicated quantity into a sum and take expectations term by term — independence not required, so it almost always works.
Use \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. It’s nearly always less work than the definition, and it makes the “squaring doesn’t commute with averaging” point concrete.
Always pair a mean with a spread. A mean without a standard deviation hides whether the average is typical or just a balance point between extremes.
Track independence before adding variances. The covariance term is where clustered or correlated data quietly inflate true uncertainty — flag it early.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Linearity, proof sketch. For discrete X,Y with joint pmf p(x,y), 
E[X+Y] = \sum_x\sum_y (x+y)\,p(x,y) = \sum_x x\sum_y p(x,y) + \sum_y y\sum_x p(x,y) = E[X] + E[Y],
 since \sum_y p(x,y) = p_X(x) and \sum_x p(x,y) = p_Y(y) are the marginals — true for any joint distribution. Scaling by constants is immediate, giving E[aX+bY]=aE[X]+bE[Y].
Computational form of the variance. Expand and use linearity: 
\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = E[X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2] = E[X^2] - 2\mu E[X] + \mu^2
= E[X^2] - \mu^2.

Variance of a linear combination. With \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y], 
\operatorname{Var}(aX+bY) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(X) + b^2\operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2ab\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
 For independent X_1,\dots,X_n (all covariances 0) with common variance \sigma^2, the average \bar X = \tfrac1n\sum X_i has \operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \sigma^2/n, hence \operatorname{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt n. Covariance and correlation get their own treatment in P8.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Connections

Builds on: Random variables — expectation and variance are summaries of a distribution.
Leads to: the common distributions (each has a signature mean and variance) and directly to the standard error in the CLT.
Deepened by: Covariance & correlation, which unpacks the covariance term in the variance-of-a-sum formula.
