title: The Law of Large Numbers
text: Average enough independent draws and the average stops wandering: it homes in on the true mean. That is the Law of Large Numbers, and it is what makes the frequentist interpretation of probability true rather than merely assumed. But it works by swamping past deviations, not by compensating for them — which is exactly why the gambler’s fallacy is a fallacy.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Why it matters
This is the theorem that closes the chapter’s loop. Way back in the interpretations entry, we said the frequentist view defines probability as a long-run frequency, and flagged that taking this as a definition is awkward. The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) repays that debt: starting only from the axioms, it proves that long-run frequencies converge to probabilities. The frequentist picture is a result, not an assumption.
It also seats the Central Limit Theorem. The LLN says where the sample average is heading (the mean); the CLT says what shape the cloud of averages takes on the way there (a bell). Together they are the theoretical engine of all of inference — and the LLN, properly understood, is also the definitive cure for one of the most stubborn errors about chance.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
The law, weak and strong
Let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent draws from the same distribution with mean \mu. Form the running average \bar X_n = \tfrac1n \sum_{i=1}^n X_i. The LLN says \bar X_n \to \mu — in two precise senses (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997):

Weak law (WLLN). For any margin \varepsilon > 0, the probability that the average is more than \varepsilon from the mean goes to zero as n \to \infty: 
P\big(|\bar X_n - \mu| > \varepsilon\big) \longrightarrow 0.
 For a specific, large n, the average is very likely close to \mu.
Strong law (SLLN). With probability 1, the entire sequence of averages converges to \mu. Stronger than the weak law: it guarantees the average gets close and stays close for all larger n, not just that any single large n is probably fine (Ross 2018).


Simulation (Grinstead & Snell). Toss a fair coin and watch the proportion of heads. At 20 tosses they got 5 heads (0.25); at 1,000 tosses, 494 heads (0.494); at 10,000 tosses, 5,039 heads (0.5039) — marching toward the theoretical 0.5 (Grinstead and Snell 1997). A fair die behaves the same way: the running average of rolls converges to E[X] = 3.5 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).



Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 10_000
flips = rng.integers(0, 2, size=n)          # 1 = heads
heads_cumsum = np.cumsum(flips)
trials = np.arange(1, n + 1)

proportion = heads_cumsum / trials
abs_dev = np.abs(heads_cumsum - trials / 2)  # |heads - n/2|

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 3.6))

axes[0].plot(trials, proportion, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1)
axes[0].axhline(0.5, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.4)
axes[0].set_xscale("log")
axes[0].set_title("Proportion of heads → 0.5")
axes[0].set_xlabel("number of tosses (log scale)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("proportion heads")
axes[0].set_ylim(0, 1)

axes[1].plot(trials, abs_dev, color="#9a6a2f", lw=1)
axes[1].set_title("Absolute deviation |heads − n/2| grows")
axes[1].set_xlabel("number of tosses")
axes[1].set_ylabel("|heads − n/2|")

fig.suptitle("The average settles while the raw gap widens", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: 10,000 fair-coin tosses. Left: the proportion of heads converges to 0.5 (the Law of Large Numbers). Right: yet the absolute deviation |heads − n/2| tends to grow. Averages stabilise by swamping early deviations, not by cancelling them.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: The tools behind the proof: Markov and Chebyshev
text:
The tools behind the proof: Markov and Chebyshev
Two inequalities bound probabilities using only a mean (Markov) or a mean and variance (Chebyshev) — remarkable because they hold for any distribution with those moments (Ross 2018).
Markov’s inequality. For a non-negative random variable, the chance it reaches a high value a is capped by its mean over a: 
P\{X \ge a\} \le \frac{E[X]}{a}.


Ross’s example. A factory averages 50 items a week. Then P\{X \ge 75\} \le 50/75 = 2/3 — the best you can say knowing only the mean (Ross 2018).

Chebyshev’s inequality. Bounds deviation from the mean using the variance: 
P\{|X - \mu| \ge k\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{k^2}.


Ross’s example. If that factory’s production has variance 25, then production lands within 10 of the mean (between 40 and 60) with probability at least 1 - 25/10^2 = 0.75 (Ross 2018).







TipHow Chebyshev proves the weak law — in one line



Apply Chebyshev to the average \bar X_n. Its mean is still \mu, but its variance is \sigma^2/n (the variance-of-a-sum result). So 
P\{|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\} \le \frac{\sigma^2/n}{\varepsilon^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2} \longrightarrow 0.
 The averaging shrinks the variance, which strangles the deviation probability. That’s the entire weak law (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997).

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: The crucial distinction: swamping, not compensating
text:
The crucial distinction: swamping, not compensating
Here is where almost everyone’s intuition goes wrong, and where the LLN earns its keep.






WarningThe gambler’s fallacy



After eight heads in a row, a tail is not “due.” The flips are independent, so the ninth is still exactly 50/50 — the coin has no memory and no debt to repay (Albers 2017).


So if the average converges to 0.5, how does it get there without tails “catching up”? By swamping, not compensating (Grinstead and Snell 1997). Suppose after some flips you’re “ahead” by 100 extra heads. The LLN does not summon 100 extra tails to cancel them. Instead, as you flip millions more times, that fixed surplus of 100 becomes a vanishing fraction of the total. The deviation isn’t erased — it’s diluted.
This produces a genuinely surprising fact:

The average stabilises while the raw gap grows. The proportion S_n/n settles toward 0.5, but the absolute deviation — the literal (heads - n/2) — tends to grow with n. Off by 10 heads in 100 flips is a 10% error; off by 500 heads in 1,000,000 flips is a larger number but only a 0.05% error (Grinstead and Snell 1997). The right panel of the figure above shows exactly this: the gap widens even as the ratio converges.

That second fact is not a footnote — it’s the seed of the CLT. The absolute deviation grows like \sqrt{n}, which is precisely why the standard error scales as \sigma/\sqrt{n} and why the sampling distribution has the width it does.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
section: What the LLN buys, and what it doesn’t
text:
What the LLN buys, and what it doesn’t
It justifies frequentist probability. Apply the strong law to an event’s indicator (1 if it happens, 0 if not): its long-run relative frequency converges, with probability 1, to P(E) (Ross 2018). This is the theorem that makes “probability = long-run frequency” consistent with the axioms rather than a separate article of faith — the promissory note from the first entry, paid in full.
It pairs with the CLT. The LLN gives the destination (the average converges to a point); the CLT gives the travel pattern (the averages scatter Normally around that point, with a width that shrinks like 1/\sqrt n) (Ross 2018). The LLN says the variance of the average vanishes; the CLT says how much probability sits within any given distance for finite n.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




The gambler’s fallacy. Believing past outcomes make future ones “due.” Independence means each trial is fresh; the law works by dilution, not by debt collection.
Confusing the average with the absolute count. The proportion converges; the raw gap (heads minus n/2) typically grows. Expecting the literal counts to equalise is the fallacy in another costume.
Expecting the LLN at small n. It is an asymptotic statement. Twenty tosses can sit far from 50% (Grinstead & Snell’s run hit 25%); “the law of small numbers” — trusting tiny samples — is a classic error the inference chapters attack directly.
Assuming convergence without finite variance. Heavy-tailed distributions (no finite variance) can break the usual guarantees — the same caveat that makes the CLT fail for long tails.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the LLN via coin-toss simulation (20 → 5 heads; 1,000 → 494; 10,000 → 5,039, proportion 0.5039) and dice averaging toward 3.5; the swamping-not-compensating intuition and the average-stabilises-while-absolute-deviation-grows point; Chebyshev as the proof tool for the weak law.
Ross, A First Course in Probability — weak vs strong law; Markov’s inequality (factory averaging 50, P\{X\ge75\}\le 2/3) and Chebyshev’s inequality (variance 25 → within 10 with probability \ge 0.75); the Chebyshev proof of the WLLN; the strong law as the justification of frequentist probability; the LLN-vs-CLT division of labour.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — the gambler’s fallacy as the misreading of independence (eight heads then a “due” tail).

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Trust averages, distrust streaks. The LLN governs the long-run average, never the next trial. Any “it’s due” reasoning is the gambler’s fallacy.
Remember “swamping, not compensating.” Deviations don’t get cancelled; they get diluted. Keeping this straight immunises you against a whole family of chance errors.
Respect small samples. Convergence is asymptotic; at small n, averages can stray far. Don’t read a stable-looking mean off twenty data points.
Use Chebyshev for a quick, assumption-light bound. When you know only a mean and variance, it gives a guaranteed (if loose) probability bound for any distribution — handy as a sanity check before invoking Normality.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Markov’s inequality. For X \ge 0 and a > 0, let \mathbf{1}_{\{X \ge a\}} be the indicator of the event \{X \ge a\}. Since X \ge 0, we have X \ge a\,\mathbf{1}_{\{X \ge a\}} (it holds with equality-or-more when X \ge a, and trivially when X < a). Taking expectations, E[X] \ge a\,P\{X \ge a\}, hence P\{X \ge a\} \le E[X]/a.
Chebyshev’s inequality. Apply Markov to the non-negative variable (X-\mu)^2 with threshold k^2: 
P\{|X-\mu| \ge k\} = P\{(X-\mu)^2 \ge k^2\} \le \frac{E[(X-\mu)^2]}{k^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{k^2}.

Weak law of large numbers. With \bar X_n = \tfrac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i, independence gives E[\bar X_n] = \mu and \operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n. Chebyshev then yields, for any \varepsilon > 0, 
P\{|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\} \le \frac{\sigma^2}{n\varepsilon^2} \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{} 0.
 This is convergence in probability. The strong law strengthens it to convergence almost surely (P\{\lim_n \bar X_n = \mu\} = 1); its proof is harder and needs more than the second moment, but the conclusion is the one that licenses defining probability as a long-run frequency.
Why the absolute deviation grows. For fair coins, S_n - n/2 has standard deviation \tfrac12\sqrt n, so the typical absolute gap grows like \sqrt n even as the proportion gap |S_n/n - \tfrac12| shrinks like 1/\sqrt n. The \sqrt n is the same one that runs through the standard error and the CLT.

title: The Law of Large Numbers
text:
Connections

Builds on: Expectation & variance (the \sigma^2/n variance of the average) and independence (which the law and the fallacy both hinge on).
Pays off the promise of: What probability is — the frequentist interpretation, now proved consistent.
Leads directly to: the flagship Sampling Distributions & the CLT, which adds the shape to the LLN’s destination and opens Part II.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text: Before you can compute a probability you must say what could happen (the sample space) and which happenings you care about (events). Events are sets, so the language of probability is the language of sets: union, intersection, complement. On top of that, three short axioms fix the rules — and almost every formula you’ll ever use is squeezed out of them.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Why it matters
This is the entry that turns probability from a vibe into a system. The three interpretations in the previous entry disagreed about meaning; they all agree about these rules. Andrey Kolmogorov’s 1933 axioms are the small, shared foundation that lets classical, frequentist, and Bayesian reasoning coexist and compute identically.
The payoff is leverage. You will not memorise the complement rule, the addition rule, or inclusion–exclusion as separate facts — you will derive them, in two lines each, from three axioms. Internalising that “everything follows from the axioms” is what makes the rest of the chapter feel inevitable instead of arbitrary.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
section: The objects: sample space and events
text:
The objects: sample space and events
The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment — written S in Ross, \Omega in Grinstead & Snell (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997). An event is any subset of the sample space: a collection of outcomes you’ve decided to group together (Ross 2018).
That second definition is the quiet key: an event is a set. “The die shows an even number” is the set \{2,4,6\}. “The respondent is satisfied or very satisfied” is a set of two response categories. Once events are sets, combining them is set algebra.









Operation
Notation
Meaning
“Occurs when…”




Union
E \cup F
outcomes in E, in F, or both

Intersection

Complement

outcomes in S not in E
E does not happen



Two events are mutually exclusive (or disjoint) if they cannot both occur — their intersection is the empty event, EF = \varnothing (Ross 2018).

Worked example (Ross). Roll two dice. Let E = “the sum is 7” and F = “the sum is 6.” A single roll can’t sum to both 6 and 7, so EF = \varnothing: these events are mutually exclusive (Ross 2018).







TipMutually exclusive ≠ independent



These two ideas get confused constantly, so plant the flag now. Mutually exclusive means the events can’t co-occur (one happening rules the other out). Independent (next entry) means one event’s occurrence tells you nothing about the other’s odds. They are nearly opposites: if two events with positive probability are mutually exclusive, then learning one occurred tells you the other definitely didn’t — that’s the strongest possible dependence, not independence.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
The three axioms
Kolmogorov’s axioms are the entire rulebook. For events in a sample space S (Ross 2018):

Non-negativity & scale: every event has a probability between 0 and 1, 0 \le P(E) \le 1.
Total mass: the sample space is certain, P(S) = 1 — something in S must happen.
Additivity: for any sequence of mutually exclusive events E_1, E_2, \dots, the probability of their union is the sum of their probabilities, 
P\!\left(\bigcup_i E_i\right) = \sum_i P(E_i).


That’s it. Read them as common sense: probabilities are fractions of a whole (axioms 1–2), and non-overlapping chances add up (axiom 3). The word “mutually exclusive” in axiom 3 is the load-bearing fine print — it’s why the general addition rule below needs a correction term.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
What follows immediately
Each of these is a short consequence of the axioms, not a new assumption (Ross 2018):

Complement rule. Since E and E^c are mutually exclusive and together fill S, axioms 2–3 give P(E) + P(E^c) = 1, so P(E^c) = 1 - P(E). (Often the fastest route to an answer: the chance of “at least one” is 1 minus the chance of “none.”)
The impossible event. P(\varnothing) = 0 — the empty set has no outcomes to contribute.
Monotonicity. If E \subset F (every way E happens is also a way F happens), then P(E) \le P(F). A bigger set can’t be less likely.
Addition rule (inclusion–exclusion for two events). For any two events — overlapping or not — 
P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) - P(EF).
 You subtract P(EF) because adding P(E) and P(F) counts the overlap twice (Ross 2018).


Worked example (Ross). Toss two fair coins; the four outcomes HH, HT, TH, TT are equally likely. Let E = “heads on the first coin” = \{HH, HT\} and F = “heads on the second” =
\{HH, TH\}. Then P(E) = P(F) = \tfrac12, but E and F overlap at HH, so 
P(E \cup F) = \tfrac12 + \tfrac12 - \tfrac14 = \tfrac34 .
 The naïve \tfrac12 + \tfrac12 = 1 is wrong precisely because it double-counts HH (Ross 2018).

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Adding probabilities of overlapping events. P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) holds only when the events are mutually exclusive. Forgetting the -P(EF) correction is the most common arithmetic error in the chapter.
Confusing “mutually exclusive” with “independent.” They are different — often opposite — ideas (see the callout above). Independence is about information, exclusivity about co-occurrence.
Mis-specifying the sample space. Half of all probability errors are really errors about S. The Chevalier de Méré (previous entry) lost money because he counted the wrong outcomes, not because he did the wrong division. Define S explicitly before you compute.
Forgetting the complement trick. Many “at least one” problems are far easier as 1 - P(\text{none}). Reaching for inclusion–exclusion when the complement is one step away wastes effort and invites mistakes.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — sample space S and events as subsets; union, intersection, complement, and mutually exclusive events (the two-dice sum-6/sum-7 example); Kolmogorov’s three axioms; the complement rule, P(\varnothing)=0, monotonicity, and the two-event addition rule (the two-coins \tfrac34 example).
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the sample space as \Omega and the set-theoretic framing of events; the axiomatic foundation shared across interpretations.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Write down S first, every time. The discipline of listing what can happen catches most errors before they start, and makes “equally likely” assumptions explicit rather than smuggled.
Reach for the complement on “at least one” problems. P(\text{at least one}) =
1 - P(\text{none}) is the highest-leverage trick in elementary probability.
Keep a mental Venn diagram. Picturing events as overlapping regions makes the -P(EF) correction obvious and stops you double-counting.
Sanity-check against the axioms. If a calculation ever yields a probability below 0, above 1, or summing past 1 over a partition, you’ve broken an axiom — stop and find the error.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Complement rule, from the axioms. E and E^c are mutually exclusive and E \cup E^c = S. By axiom 3, P(E) + P(E^c) = P(S), and by axiom 2, P(S) = 1. Hence P(E^c) = 1 - P(E). Setting E = S gives P(\varnothing) = 1 - P(S) = 0.
Monotonicity. If E \subset F, write F as the disjoint union F = E \cup (F \cap E^c). Axiom 3 gives P(F) = P(E) + P(F \cap E^c) \ge P(E), since the second term is \ge 0 by axiom 1.
Addition rule (two events). Decompose each set into disjoint pieces: 
E \cup F = (E \cap F^c) \cup (E \cap F) \cup (E^c \cap F),
 a union of three mutually exclusive events. Applying axiom 3 to this union, and also to E = (E \cap F^c) \cup (E \cap F) and F = (E^c \cap F) \cup (E \cap F), then eliminating the piece probabilities, yields 
P(E \cup F) = P(E) + P(F) - P(E \cap F).

Inclusion–exclusion (three events). The same bookkeeping extended one level: 
P(E \cup F \cup G) = P(E)+P(F)+P(G) - P(EF) - P(EG) - P(FG) + P(EFG).
 The alternating pattern — add singles, subtract pairs, add the triple — continues for any number of events, and is the general inclusion–exclusion principle.

title: Sample Spaces, Events & the Axioms
text:
Connections

Builds on: What probability is — the axioms are the rulebook all three interpretations share.
Leads to: Counting — when outcomes are equally likely, probabilities are ratios of set sizes, so computing them is counting.
Pays off in: Conditional probability & independence, where the addition rule meets its multiplicative cousin, and everywhere a probability is manipulated thereafter.

title: II · Inference
text: Inference is the discipline of saying something about a population you cannot fully see, using a sample you can, while being honest about the uncertainty. Every tool here is a way of reading the sampling distribution — the idea that turns a single number into a claim.

A recurring theme
This part carries the manual’s critical conscience: Statistics Done Wrong sits beside every test, because knowing how inference is misused (p-hacking, optional stopping, underpowered studies) is part of knowing how to use it.

title: II · Inference
text:
Where this leads
Inference is the grammar that Part III · Regression speaks: a regression coefficient is an estimate, and everything you learn here about uncertainty attaches to it.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text: This is where statistics meets the social world’s questions. The centre of gravity is regression: a tool for holding things constant, summarising relationships, and — with care — estimating effects. The undergraduate core: OLS, its assumptions, its inference, its honest diagnostics, and the limited-dependent-variable models for outcomes that aren’t continuous.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
The bridge to causality
Mastering ’Metrics frames regression as a way of making “other things equal.” That framing is the hinge between description and Causal Inference: a coefficient is only an effect if you have an argument for why the comparison is fair.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
Where this leads
Onward to Part IV · Predictive Modelling — the computational turn, where the goal shifts from explaining to predicting, and the same regression machinery is judged by a completely different standard.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text: The summit of the whole manual, and the centre of gravity of the source library. The question is deceptively simple — what is the effect of X on Y? — and answering it honestly requires a framework for imagining the world that didn’t happen, plus the design and computational tools built in every part before this one.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The “Furious Five”
The applied core, as Angrist & Pischke frame it: randomised experiments, regression adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences. Each is a different argument for why a comparison is fair — a different way of approximating the experiment you wish you could have run.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
Where this leads
Down from the summit to Part VI · Doing Research in the World: having learned what a credible causal claim is, you learn what survives contact with measurement, incentives, and a client who needs an answer by Friday.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
Machine learning is kept deliberately separate from the causal main track, and bridged to it only where it earns a place. The reason is the argument made in Predictive Modelling: ML is, by default, the predictive culture. Pretending its tools answer causal questions is one of the most common and costly confusions in applied work.
That said, the frontier of social science increasingly uses ML in service of causal goals — estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity, handling high-dimensional controls — and this track follows that bridge carefully.

The bridge back
Every entry here points back to the main spine: a prediction is not an effect, and a feature importance is not a cause. The ML track sharpens your tools without loosening your causal discipline.
